title: Evolutionary Psychology — Overview
content_html:
Evolutionary Psychology — Overview

Prompt (saved): “Evolutionary psychology → HTML export with collapsible sections, citations, and links.” Timestamp: 2025-09-09 13:11:42 BST Context: Memory project — export-ready for Apple Notes & GitHub (HTML primary).

This overview frames evolutionary psychology for your Memory project and Processing application notes. Sections expand/collapse for quick scanning.

Core Principles

  • Natural selection applies to cognition: psychological traits can be adaptations shaped by selection.
  • Functional specialization: the mind comprises many problem-specific mechanisms (often called “modules”).
  • Environment of Evolutionary Adaptedness (EEA): many mechanisms evolved in hunter–gatherer contexts; modern life can create mismatches (e.g., sugar preference).

Good primers: Stanford Encyclopedia overview; Cosmides & Tooby’s Primer.

Key Research Areas

  • Mating & attraction: cross-cultural regularities and sex-differentiated strategies.
  • Parental investment: different reproductive costs shape strategies (Trivers 1972).
  • Cooperation & morality: kin selection, reciprocal altruism, and cheater detection.
  • Emotion as adaptation: fear, disgust, jealousy, love as fitness-relevant programs.

Strengths

  • Unifies psychology with evolutionary biology.
  • Generates testable, cross-cultural predictions.
  • Explains broad regularities in human behavior.

Critiques & Debates

  • Adaptationism: over-attributing traits to selection vs. byproducts/culture.
  • “Just-so” hypotheses: prehistoric narratives can be difficult to falsify.
  • Culture & learning: concern that cultural variation is underweighted.
  • Scientific status debates: boundary questions with sociobiology and philosophy of science.

Links to Your Project Themes

  • Memory & future planning: episodic memory may have evolved for prospective simulation (planning), aligning with your “remembering forward” theme.
  • Select – Change – Reconstruct: adaptive attention (select), plasticity/learning (change), and reconstructive recall (reconstruct) fit EP’s functional lens.
  • Processing app notes: when designing interfaces or visualizations about memory, consider evolved biases (e.g., salience for faces, threats, kin signals).

Recommended Readings (with links)

  • Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy: Evolutionary Psychology
  • Cosmides & Tooby (1993/updated) — Evolutionary Psychology: A Primer (PDF)
  • Buss (2019), Evolutionary Psychology: The New Science of the Mind (Routledge page)
  • Trivers (1972) — Parental Investment & Sexual Selection (PDF)
  • Stanford Encyclopedia: Sociobiology (context & debates)

APA-Style References

  1. Buss, D. M. (2019). Evolutionary psychology: The new science of the mind (6th ed.). Routledge. https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9780429061417/evolutionary-psychology-david-buss
  2. Cosmides, L., & Tooby, J. (1993). Evolutionary psychology: A primer. (Updated PDF). Center for Evolutionary Psychology, UCSB. https://www.cep.ucsb.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/Evolutionary-Psychology-A-Primer-CosmidesTooby1993.pdf
  3. Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). Evolutionary psychology. https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/evolutionary-psychology/
  4. Trivers, R. L. (1972). Parental investment and sexual selection. In B. Campbell (Ed.), Sexual selection and the descent of man, 1871–1971 (pp. 136–179). Aldine. https://joelvelasco.net/teaching/3330/trivers72-parentalinvestment.pdf
  5. Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). Sociobiology. https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/sociobiology/
